A large number of Arab and Persian merchants then settled in China, forming 'Fanfang' (Foreign Quarters). Islam entered China via the overland Silk Road and maritime spice routes, becoming one of the foreign religions in China.

v.t: Arabs completely destroy the Sasanian Empire
v.s: The last Persian king, Yazdegerd III, was killed by a miller at Merv. His son Peroz fled to the Tang court seeking refuge. Arab forces gained full control of the Persian plateau. Zoroastrian temples were converted to mosques, and many Zoroastrians converted to Islam. Persian nobles and scholars gradually integrated into the Arab Empire, but Persian culture and language (especially New Persian) persisted, laying the foundation for later Persian-Islamic civilization.

v.t: Completion of the 'Tang Code Commentary'
v.s: Emperor Gaozong ordered Changsun Wuji to lead the production of a commentary to the Zhenguan Code, resulting in the thirty-volume 'Tang Code Commentary' (Tanglü Shuyi). The commentary explains the meaning and application of each legal article and carries the same legal force as the code. The 'Tang Code Commentary' is the oldest extant complete Chinese legal code, known for its rigorous structure and clear logic. It became the model for later imperial Chinese law codes and profoundly influenced the legal systems of Japan, Korea, Vietnam, and other East Asian countries, earning it the title 'cornerstone of the East Asian legal family'.

v.t: Tang forces defeat Ashina Helu; Western Turks surrender
v.s: Emperor Gaozong ordered the great general Su Dingfang to lead an army against the Western Turkic khan Ashina Helu. Su Dingfang won decisive victories at the Altai Mountains (Jinshan) and the Jinya Mountains (present-day Emin, Xinjiang), capturing Helu. The remaining Western Turkic tribes surrendered. The Tang established the Mengchi and Kunling Protectorates on the former Western Turkic territory to govern the ten tribes. The Western Turkic Khaganate was destroyed, and Tang achieved suzerainty over Central Asia.

v.t: Rise of the Umayyad clan in the Arab Empire
v.s: Caliph Uthman appointed many of his Umayyad relatives to high positions, such as Mu'awiya as governor of Syria. The power of the Umayyad clan expanded greatly, causing discontent among other tribes, particularly the Hashimites and the supporters of Ali. This internal conflict led to Uthman's assassination in 656 and the first Islamic civil war (Battle of Siffin), laying the power base for the subsequent Umayyad caliphate.

v.t: Xuanzang translates Buddhist scriptures back in Chang'an
v.s: Monk Xuanzang, after returning to Chang'an, established a translation bureau at Daci'en Temple and translated a large number of Sanskrit Buddhist texts. He produced translations of important Yogācāra works such as the Yogācārabhūmi-śāstra and the Chengweishi-lun, founding the Faxiang (Yogācāra) school of Buddhism. Xuanzang's translations, known for their accuracy and fluency, greatly enriched the Chinese Buddhist canon. He also translated Chinese classics, such as the 'Tao Te Ching', into Sanskrit for transmission to India, promoting cultural exchange.

v.t: Chen Shuozhen's rebellion
v.s: Chen Shuozhen, a woman from Qingxi (present-day Chun'an, Zhejiang), used Daoist-Maitreyan doctrines to organize a peasant rebellion, proclaiming herself 'Wenjia Emperor' (Civilizing Emperor), the first female rebel leader to claim the title of emperor in Chinese history. She led her followers to capture Mu Prefecture and Tonglu, shaking the southeast. Emperor Gaozong sent Cui Yixuan to suppress the uprising. Chen was captured and executed. The rebellion highlighted the social contradictions of heavy peasant burdens and unequal tax and labor policies under the equal-field system.

v.t: Wu Zetian re-enters the palace
v.s: Emperor Gaozong, while visiting Ganye Temple, met Wu Zetian (formerly a consort of his father Emperor Taizong), who had become a nun there. Their old passion reignited. Overriding opposition from Empress Wang and ministers such as Changsun Wuji, Gaozong brought Wu back into the palace and made him a zhaoyin (a high-rank consort). After her return, Wu quickly cultivated power in the palace and court through cunning and machination, engaging in fierce rivalry with Empress Wang and Consort Xiao, setting the stage for her eventual replacement of Wang and subsequent interference in politics.

v.t: Wu Zetian is made empress
v.s: Gaozong deposed Empress Wang and Consort Xiao as commoners and made Wu Zetian empress. In the power struggle, Wu had plotted against Wang (according to the 'Zizhi Tongjian', she killed her own daughter and blamed Wang). She also accused Wang and Xiao of practicing witchcraft. The senior statesmen led by Changsun Wuji and Chu Suiliang strongly opposed, but Gaozong accepted Li Ji's remark that 'this is your majesty's family affair' and issued an edict making Wu empress. Empress Wang and Consort Xiao were later killed. After becoming empress, Wu quickly began interfering in affairs of state, forming a 'Two Saints' power structure.

v.t: First Arab attack on Constantinople
v.s: Caliph Uthman ordered Mu'awiya, governor of Syria, to lead a combined naval and land force against the Byzantine capital, Constantinople. The Arab fleet sailed from Syria along the coast of Anatolia, but encountered Byzantine 'Greek fire' for the first time on a large scale and suffered heavy losses. On land, the Arabs were repulsed by Constantinople's massive walls and the cold winter, forcing a withdrawal. This was the first major Arab assault on Constantinople, and although it failed, it foreshadowed centuries of continued threat.

v.t: Third Caliph Uthman is assassinated
v.s: Due to Uthman's nepotism, corruption, and the forced unification of the Quran and burning of variant copies, widespread discontent arose among Muslims, especially in Egypt and Kufa. Five hundred rebels besieged Uthman's house in Medina and eventually killed him. Uthman's assassination marked the end of the Rashidun Caliphate and the first major split within the Muslim community. Ali was proclaimed the fourth caliph, but Mu'awiya, governor of Syria, refused to recognize him, leading to the first Islamic civil war (Battle of Siffin) and the formal split between Sunni and Shia.

v.t: Cheng Yaojin campaigns against the Western Turks
v.s: Emperor Gaozong ordered Cheng Yaojin (Cheng Zhijie) to lead an army against the Western Turkic remnants under Ashina Helu. Cheng Yaojin fought the Western Turks at Yingsuo River (present-day Bayinbuluk, Xinjiang), killing over a thousand. However, jealousy from his deputy Wang Wendu led to a premature withdrawal, losing the chance for a decisive victory. Cheng Yaojin was dismissed, and Su Dingfang took over command.

v.t: Gaozong changes era name to Xianqing; Wu Zetian begins participating in politics
v.s: Emperor Gaozong changed the era name to Xianqing, and Empress Wu Zetian began participating in imperial decision-making. The senior ministers Changsun Wuji and Chu Suiliang were successively dismissed and exiled. Taking advantage of Gaozong's frequent illness, Wu gradually built her own political team (such as Xu Jingzong and Li Yifu). During the Xianqing years, a pattern of 'emperor and empress co-ruling' emerged, with Wu effectively acting as a second sovereign, preparing the way for her later claim to the throne.

v.t: Tang destroys the Western Turks
v.s: Su Dingfang was appointed commander for the Ili route (Yili Dao). He led 10,000 elite cavalry along with Uyghur and other allied horse archers against Ashina Helu. Su Dingfang defeated Helu at the Ili River and the Chu River (Suyab), pursuing him to Tashkent (Shiguo), where the lord of Tashkent captured Helu and sent him to the Tang camp. The Western Turkic Khaganate was completely destroyed. The Tang established the Kunling and Mengchi Protectorates on its territory, appointing the ten tribal leaders as governors (duhu). The Tang western frontier extended to the Aral Sea, bordering Persia and the Arab Empire.

v.t: Ali becomes the fourth caliph
v.s: After Uthman's assassination, the Muslims of Medina elected Ali ibn Abi Talib (Muhammad's cousin and son-in-law) as the fourth Rashidun caliph. Ali tried to curb the power of Mu'awiya, governor of Syria, but Mu'awiya refused to submit, demanding punishment for Uthman's murder. The two sides faced off on the plain of Siffin, and after negotiations broke down, civil war erupted. As a result, Islam split into the pro-Ali Shia (meaning 'party') and the majority Sunni.

v.t: The Anxi Protectorate moves its seat to Kucha
v.s: The Tang moved the seat of the Anxi Protectorate from Xi Prefecture to Kucha (present-day Kuqa, Xinjiang), formally establishing the Anxi Four Garrisons (Kucha, Kashgar, Khotan, and Karashahr). The Anxi Protectorate became the Tang military and political center for the Western Regions, controlling the southern Tian Shan route, the Pamirs, and the Transoxiana region, and serving as a vital security guarantee for the Silk Road.

v.t: Changsun Wuji is exiled; Gaozong centralizes power
v.s: Wu Zetian and Xu Jingzong conspired to fabricate a rebellion charge against Changsun Wuji. Emperor Gaozong exiled Changsun Wuji to Qianzhou, and soon after Xu Jingzong forced him to commit suicide. Changsun Wuji, the senior minister entrusted by Taizong, fell, marking the complete collapse of the Guanlong aristocratic group, and Gaozong and Wu Zetian gained full control of the court. Afterward, Wu promoted commoner scholars and those from humble backgrounds, leading to profound changes in the bureaucracy.

v.t: The Tang Anxi Protectorate expands further west
v.s: After destroying the Western Turks, the Tang continued to expand westward. The jurisdiction of the Anxi Protectorate extended west of the Pamirs (present-day Pamir Plateau), including the nine states of Zhaowu (Sogdian states such as Samarkand, Bukhara, and Tashkent). The Tang established Jimi prefectures and sub-prefectures in these areas and appointed local kings as governors (duhu). Tang's sphere of influence reached eastern Persia, directly confronting the Arab Empire.

v.t: Su Dingfang destroys Baekje
v.s: At the request of Silla, Emperor Gaozong ordered Su Dingfang to lead 100,000 land and naval forces against Baekje. Su Dingfang set sail from Chengshan (Rongcheng, Shandong) and landed at the mouth of the Baek River (Geum River), defeated the Baekje army, and besieged its capital, Sabi. King Uija of Baekje surrendered, and Baekje was destroyed. The Tang established five governor-general offices (Bear River, Mahan, Dongmyeong, etc.) on Baekje's former territory, with local chieftains serving as governors. Later, the Baekje restoration movement led by Gwisil Boksin sought Japanese aid, leading to the Battle of Baekgang in 663.

v.t: Establishment of the Umayyad Caliphate with capital at Damascus
v.s: After Ali was assassinated at Kufa, Mu'awiya defeated Ali's successor Hasan and became the sole caliph, founding the Umayyad Caliphate. He moved the capital to Damascus and replaced the elective system with hereditary monarchy. Mu'awiya pursued Arabization policies: issuing a unified gold dinar coin, making Arabic the official language, and establishing a postal service and bureaucracy. The Umayyad Caliphate expanded from Spain to Central Asia, becoming one of the largest empires of its time.

v.t: Tang, at Silla's request, jointly attacks Goguryeo
v.s: King Muyeol of Silla sent an envoy to the Tang requesting aid in attacking Goguryeo. Emperor Gaozong ordered Qibi Heli, Su Dingfang, and others to lead forces against Goguryeo. The Tang captured the important western fortress of Liaodong, but due to logistical difficulties and lingering issues in Baekje, failed to destroy Goguryeo in one stroke. This joint campaign was a prelude to the final Tang conquest of Goguryeo in 668.

v.t: Prelude to the Battle of Baekgang: Baekje restoration movement
v.s: The Baekje restoration leader Gwisil Boksin established Prince Buyeo Pung as king, holding the fortress of Juryu, and requested aid from Japan. Empress Saimei (Empress Kōgyoku) of Japan decided to send troops to help restore Baekje, dispatching Abe no Hirafu with a fleet. The Tang, for their part, sent Liu Rengui and Liu Renyuan to lead Tang forces stationed in former Baekje territory to suppress the restorationists. The two sides confronted each other in the southwestern part of the Korean Peninsula, with battle imminent.

v.t: Arab forces attack Anatolia
v.s: Umayyad Caliph Mu'awiya sent forces deep into Byzantine Asia Minor, capturing several fortresses and raiding towns. The Byzantine forces used guerrilla tactics and Greek fire to resist, but annual Arab summer incursions gradually depopulated the Greek population of Anatolia, creating conditions for later Turkish settlement.

v.t: Battle of Baekgang: Tang navy decisively defeats the joint Japanese–Baekje fleet
v.s: Tang admiral Liu Rengui led 7,000 marines and 170 warships into battle at the Baekgang River (Geum River estuary) against the Japanese (Wa) fleet. The Japanese had over 400 ships and more than 30,000 men. Liu used the superior height and firepower of the Tang ships, along with fire attacks, surrounding the Japanese fleet from four directions. The crowded Japanese ships were engulfed in flames, turning the sea red. The Battle of Baekgang was a famous naval engagement in ancient Chinese history, completely crushing Japanese ambitions to intervene on the Korean peninsula and establishing Tang hegemony in Northeast Asia. Afterward, Japan submitted fully and intensified the dispatch of missions to Tang China to learn its systems.

v.t: Xuanzang passes away
v.s: The monk Xuanzang died in the Jade Flower Temple in Chang'an at the age of sixty-three. Emperor Gaozong, deeply saddened, ordered the suspension of court for five days and that the burial expenses be borne by the state. Xuanzang was buried at White Deer Plain, later moved to Shaoling Plain. He had translated 75 texts in 1,335 volumes, written the 'Great Tang Records on the Western Regions', and founded the Faxiang (Yogācāra) school. Xuanzang's spirit of seeking the Dharma became a symbol of Sino-Indian cultural exchange and had an incalculable impact on the development of Chinese Buddhism.

v.t: Arabs advance west into North Africa
v.s: The Umayyad Caliphate sent Uqba ibn Nafi leading Arab cavalry westward. He crossed the Libyan desert, captured Tripoli, and continued west into modern-day Tunisia. Uqba founded the city of Kairouan (in modern Tunisia) as a base for Islam in the Maghreb. The Great Mosque of Kairouan became the center of Islam in North Africa.

v.t: First Tang-Tibetan peace talks
v.s: Tibetan lönchen (grand minister) Gar Tongtsen (also known as Mgar Stong-rtsan) had multiple talks with the Tang regarding borders and marriage alliances before the Battle of Dafeichuan (670). In 665, envoys met to discuss the boundary west of Songzhou (present-day Songpan, Sichuan), but failed to reach an agreement. The Tibetans continued to encroach on the Tang's Anxi Four Garrisons and the former Tuyuhun territory, with increasing tensions.

v.t: Emperor Gaozong plans another campaign against Goguryeo
v.s: Emperor Gaozong appointed Li Ji (Li Shiji) as commander-in-chief of the Liaodong route, planning a large-scale campaign against Goguryeo. Li Ji adopted a strategy of 'protracted war,' first taking the eastern fortresses of Goguryeo and cutting off its external support, preparing the way for the final conquest in 668. The Tang–Goguryeo war entered its final phase.

v.t: Emperor Gaozong of Tang conducts a Fengshan sacrifice at Mount Tai
v.s: Emperor Gaozong of Tang, following the examples of the First Emperor of Qin and Emperor Wu of Han, led a large entourage of officials and foreign envoys to Mount Tai to perform the Fengshan ceremony. Fengshan was the highest imperial ritual of sacrificing to heaven and earth at Mount Tai, announcing that the emperor had received the Mandate of Heaven. Gaozong first offered sacrifice to heaven on the sealing altar, then to earth on the revetment altar. Empress Wu Zetian, as empress, performed the second sacrifice (the first time in history). The ceremony was extremely costly but demonstrated the prosperity of the Tang and the homage of the Four Directions.

v.t: Arab fleet again attacks Byzantium
v.s: Umayyad Caliph Mu'awiya sent naval forces from Syria to attack Byzantine coastal cities. The Byzantine navy repelled the Arab fleet with Greek fire. The Arabs, however, gained control of the eastern Mediterranean, capturing the islands of Rhodes and Kos and conducting annual raids against Constantinople.

v.t: Emperor Tenji of Japan ascends the throne
v.s: After the death of Empress Saimei, Prince Naka no Ōe ascended the throne as Emperor Tenji. He continued to implement the Taika Reforms, promulgating the 'Ōmi Code' (Japan's first written legal code), perfecting the bureaucratic and household registration systems, and revising the 'Kōgō Nenjaku' (a census). Emperor Tenji also commissioned the compilation of Japan's first national history, the 'Nihon Shoki' (completed in 720). His reign marked further consolidation of a law-based state in Japan.

v.t: Li Ji captures the 'New City' of Goguryeo
v.s: Tang general Li Ji, in the campaign against Goguryeo, captured 'New City' (present-day Fushun Golsan, Liaoning), an important western fortress of Goguryeo. Its fall opened the western gateway to Goguryeo, allowing Tang forces to advance deep into enemy territory. Li Ji then captured Jinshan (present-day Kaiyuan, Liaoning), linking up with the forces of Xue Rengui, forming a pincer movement against the Goguryeo capital Pyongyang.

v.t: Arab forces under the Umayyad Caliphate invade Asia Minor
v.s: The Arab general Mu'awiya (later caliph) led his army from Syria into Byzantine Asia Minor, occupying Cappadocia and threatening the Asiatic side of Constantinople. The Byzantine Emperor Constans II was forced to move his capital to Sicily, but died shortly thereafter. The Arab offensive paused for a time.

v.t: Tang destroys Goguryeo
v.s: Tang forces under Li Ji and Xue Rengui linked up and besieged the Goguryeo capital Pyongyang. King Bojang surrendered, and the powerful Dae Mangniji Yeon Gaesomun had already died; his son Yeon Namsaeng was captured. Goguryeo, which had existed for 705 years (37 BC – 668 AD), was destroyed. The Tang established the Protectorate General to Pacify the East (Andong Protectorate) at Pyongyang, appointing Xue Rengui as Protector-General (Andong Duhu), governing nine governorates and forty-two prefectures of the former Goguryeo. Hundreds of thousands of Goguryeo people were resettled in the Jianghuai and Central Plains regions, gradually integrating into the Han population. The destruction of Goguryeo brought Tang power in Northeast Asia to its peak.

v.t: Silla unifies the region south of the Daedong River on the Korean Peninsula
v.s: After the Tang destroyed Goguryeo, Silla seized the former Goguryeo territory south of the Daedong River (the southern part of the Korean Peninsula). King Munmu of Silla (Kim Beopmin) sought to unify the entire peninsula, leading to conflict with the Tang. The Tang was compelled to recognize Silla's control over the former Baekje territories and part of southern Goguryeo, and Silla entered the 'Unified Silla' period. The Tang, however, retained the Andong Protectorate to control Liaodong and the northern part of the Korean Peninsula.

v.t: Tibet invades Tang borders on a large scale
v.s: Tibetan lönchen Gar Tongtsen took advantage of the Tang's main forces being committed in Liaodong and attacked the Anxi Four Garrisons (Kucha, Kashgar, Khotan, Karashahr), shaking Tang control over the Western Regions. At the same time, Tibetan forces occupied the former Tuyuhun territory, directly threatening the Hexi Corridor. Tang-Tibetan relations deteriorated sharply, setting the stage for the Battle of Dafeichuan in 670.

v.t: Tang undertakes massive resettlement of Goguryeo remnants
v.s: To prevent Goguryeo remnants from trying to restore their kingdom, the Tang moved hundreds of thousands of Goguryeo nobles and commoners in batches to the Jianghuai, Central Plains, and Sichuan regions. The Goguryeo royal family were given official positions and scattered in Chang'an and other places. This resettlement effectively broke the cohesion of the Goguryeo people, but also led to the widespread loss of Goguryeo culture and historical records.

v.t: Battle of Dafeichuan: Tang army suffers a disastrous defeat by Tibet
v.s: Tibetan lönchen Gar Trinring (also known as Mgar Khri-'bring) led 400,000 troops to attack the Tuyuhun. The Tang sent Xue Rengui as commander-in-chief of the Lhasa route (Luo suo dao) with 50,000 troops. Xue Rengui first launched a cavalry raid, defeating the Tibetans at Dafeichuan (southwest of present-day Gonghe, Qinghai). However, his deputy Guo Daifeng disobeyed orders, and his supply train was captured by the Tibetans. The Tang army, out of food, retreated and was surrounded at Dafeichuan by the main Tibetan force, resulting in total annihilation. Xue Rengui was forced to conclude a peace and return. The Battle of Dafeichuan was one of the greatest military defeats for the Tang since its founding, and thereafter the Tibetans faced the Tang along the Hexi Corridor in a standoff.

v.t: The Dome of the Rock is completed in Jerusalem
v.s: The Umayyad caliph Abd al-Malik built the Dome of the Rock on the Temple Mount in Jerusalem (known to Muslims as Haram al-Sharif). The octagonal building covers a large rock believed to be the spot from which Muhammad ascended to heaven, and also where Abraham was to sacrifice Isaac. The dome was originally covered in gold leaf, and the interior has splendid mosaics. The Dome of the Rock became the third holiest site in Islam (after the Kaaba in Mecca and the Prophet's Mosque in Medina), as well as a landmark of Jerusalem.

v.t: First Arab siege of Constantinople
v.s: Umayyad Caliph Mu'awiya sent his son Yazid with land and naval forces to besiege Constantinople. The Arab fleet blockaded the Bosphorus, and the land assault continued for several years. Byzantine Emperor Constantine IV used Greek fire and the city's massive walls to hold out. The siege continued until 678, when it was finally lifted. This was one of the most dangerous Arab attacks on Constantinople.

v.t: Cultural influence of the Turkic Khaganate in Central Asia
v.s: During its rule over Central Asia, the Second Eastern Turkic Khaganate (or later Türkic Khaganate) absorbed Persian and Sogdian elements, created the Old Turkic runic script (Orkhon-Yenisei script), and erected the famous 'Kul Tigin Stele' and 'Bilge Khagan Stele'. Inscribed in both Old Turkic and Chinese, these stelae record Turkic history, heroic stories, and interactions with the Tang, making them precious sources for the study of the ancient Turkic language and history.

v.t: Sailendra dynasty rules Java
v.s: The Sailendra dynasty (Shailendra) of central Java began to rise. The dynasty adhered to Mahayana Buddhism, and based on agriculture and maritime trade, built large Buddhist temple complexes including Borobudur. The Sailendra dynasty competed with the Srivijaya Empire for control of the Strait of Malacca, later forming an alliance through marriage.

v.t: Greek fire repels the Arab fleet
v.s: The Byzantine navy used Greek fire (a liquid incendiary compound of petroleum, sulfur, and other ingredients that burns on water) on a large scale in the naval battle before Constantinople. The Arab fleet was heavily damaged by Greek fire, losing many ships. The successful use of Greek fire helped Byzantium defend its capital and forced the Arabs to withdraw. This is the most famous historical example of Greek fire in action.

v.t: Umayyad expansion into Central Asia
v.s: The Arab general Sa'id ibn Uthman led his army east across the Amu Darya, capturing the Central Asian cities of Samarkand and Bukhara and defeating the Sogdian states (Zhaowu Nine Surnames), allies of the Tang. Arab power now directly confronted the Tang Anxi Protectorate in Central Asia, setting the stage for the later Battle of Talas in 751.

v.t: Tang and Tibet again hold peace talks
v.s: After the defeat at Dafeichuan, the Tang sent envoys to Tibet to negotiate with Gar Trinring. The talks centered on the Tuyuhun territory, but Tibet made excessive demands, and the negotiations failed. Thereafter, the Tang revised its frontier strategy, abandoning the Tuyuhun and concentrating its defenses on the Hexi Corridor.

v.t: Rise of the Srivijaya Empire
v.s: The Srivijaya (Sanfoqi) king Jayanaga established maritime hegemony at Palembang in Sumatra, controlling the Strait of Malacca. Srivijaya established tributary relations with the Tang and sent many missions to China. The kingdom was the most powerful maritime empire in Southeast Asia from the 7th to the 11th century and a center of Buddhist propagation.

v.t: Umayyads continue expansion into the Maghreb
v.s: Uqba ibn Nafi led his army from Kairouan west to the Atlantic coast of present-day Morocco. He captured Tangier and subdued the Berber tribes. The Arab Empire reached the Atlantic for the first time. However, on his return march, Uqba was killed by the Berber chieftain Kusayla, temporarily halting the Arab conquest.

v.t: Mu'awiya continues the siege of Constantinople
v.s: Umayyad caliph Mu'awiya continued to send troops to besiege Constantinople, but the Byzantine land walls and Greek fire prevented the Arabs from achieving a decisive breakthrough. Byzantine Emperor Constantine IV also launched counterattacks, harassing the Arab rear. The siege became a stalemate.

v.t: Tang suppresses the Goguryeo restoration movement
v.s: Remnants of Goguryeo led by Geom Mojam launched a rebellion in an attempt to restore the kingdom. Emperor Gaozong ordered Gao Kan, the Andong Protector-General, to suppress it. Tang forces crushed Geom Mojam and captured several thousand of his followers. Thereafter, the Tang strengthened control over Goguryeo remnants, dispersing them inland, and the Goguryeo restoration movement was completely crushed.

v.t: Arab empire continues its siege of Constantinople
v.s: The Arab forces continued the siege of Constantinople without being able to breach the walls. The Byzantine navy repeatedly sortied, using Greek fire against the Arab fleet. Both sides suffered heavy losses, and the battle was stalemated. This was a crucial engagement that decided the fate of Europe: the stubborn defense of Constantinople blocked the expansion of Islam into Europe.

v.t: Umayyad monetary reforms
v.s: Caliph Abd al-Malik introduced monetary reform, abolishing Byzantine and Persian coins and minting the gold dinar (4.25g pure gold) and silver dirham. Coins no longer bore the portrait of a monarch, but instead were decorated with Quranic verses. The reform unified the monetary system of the Arab Empire, promoted internal trade and prosperity, and marked the cultural separation of the Arab Empire from Byzantium and Persia.

v.t: Wu Zetian is proclaimed 'Empress of Heaven', establishing a 'Two Saints' rule
v.s: Due to Emperor Gaozong's severe dizziness and inability to govern, all court business was first decided by Empress Wu Zetian. Wu took advantage of this to add the title 'Empress of Heaven' (Tianhou), and she and Gaozong were known as the 'Two Saints'. Wu Zetian began to openly participate in the highest decision-making, appointing her own trusted followers as chancellors and effectively taking control of the court. This was a key step on Wu's path to the imperial throne.

v.t: Arabs conquer the remaining Byzantine territories in North Africa
v.s: The Arab general Hassan ibn al-Nu'man led his army to capture Carthage, completely destroying the last Byzantine stronghold in North Africa. A Byzantine fleet briefly retook Carthage, but the Arabs permanently held it. The Arabs established firm control over North Africa and began advancing westward to the Strait of Gibraltar, preparing for the invasion of Spain in 711.

v.t: Emperor Gaozong considers abdicating to Wu Zetian, but abandons the plan
v.s: When Emperor Gaozong was seriously ill, he considered abdicating in favor of Wu Zetian, allowing her to hold court. The chancellors Hao Chujun and Li Yiyan strongly opposed, citing that 'the empire belongs to the ancestors Gaozu and Taizong'. Gaozong therefore abandoned the idea. However, Wu Zetian's power continued to grow, and no one at court could stop her.

v.t: Tang moves the Andong Protectorate to Liaodong
v.s: Due to Silla's expansion and insufficient Tang forces, the Tang moved the Andong Protectorate from Pyongyang east to Liaodong (present-day Liaoyang), and later to Youzhou (Beijing). This marked the effective abandonment of direct Tang control over the northern Korean Peninsula. Silla effectively occupied the region south of the Daedong River. The Tang and Silla maintained a nominal suzerain-vassal relationship but no longer fought each other.

v.t: Japan compiles the 'Asuka Kiyomihara Code'
v.s: Emperor Tenmu of Japan (673-686) began compiling the 'Asuka Kiyomihara Code' (also called the 'Tenmu Code'), Japan's first complete legal code, covering administrative, criminal, civil, and other norms. Although the original has been lost, it provided the foundation for the later 'Taihō Code' (701), promoting the development of Japan as a law-based state.

v.t: Tibet captures the Anxi Four Garrisons
v.s: Tibetan lönchen Gar Trinring led forces to capture the Tang's Anxi Four Garrisons (Kucha, Kashgar, Khotan, Karashahr), causing Tang rule over the Western Regions to collapse. Emperor Gaozong ordered Xiao Siye to lead a western expedition, but he could not recover the garrisons. The Anxi Protectorate was moved back to Xi Prefecture (Gaochang). Tibet controlled the Western Regions for about a decade, until they were recaptured by Wang Xiaojie during the Wu Zhou period.

v.t: Arab armies advance into Spain (Visigothic Kingdom)
v.s: The Arab Empire sent a Berber general, Tariq ibn Ziyad, from North Africa across the Strait of Gibraltar into the Iberian Peninsula. Although the main conquest began in 711, the Arabs had already carried out reconnaissance and raids before. Some Jews and nobles welcomed the Arabs due to discontent with Visigothic rule.

v.t: Li Jingxuan's campaign against Tibet ends in disaster
v.s: Emperor Gaozong appointed Li Jingxuan as commander-in-chief of the Taohe route, leading 180,000 Tang troops to attack Tibet. Li Jingxuan, who knew nothing of military affairs, fought the Tibetans near Qinghai Lake and was utterly routed; his deputy Liu Shenli was captured. The Tang army suffered heavy losses. Only when the general Heichi Changzhi led a night raid on the Tibetan camp with a few hundred elite horsemen did the Tibetans withdraw. This defeat further weakened Tang defenses in the Hexi region.

v.t: Tang strengthens its northern frontier defenses
v.s: In response to threats from Tibet and the Turks, the Tang constructed extensive fortifications on the northern and northwestern frontiers, including the three Shouxiang Forts (East, Central, West). At the same time, the Tang increased its efforts to appease tribes such as the Khitans and Xis in the northeast, in order to divide Turkic power.

v.t: The former Baekje prince Buyeo Yung dies
v.s: After the destruction of Goguryeo, Buyeo Yung (also known as Buyeo Yung), the last crown prince of Baekje, was appointed by the Tang as the governor of Ungjin to rule over the Baekje remnants. Seeing no hope of restoration and having a poor relationship with Tang generals, he eventually died in Luoyang. His death marked the end of any effective leadership for the Baekje restoration movement, and the Baekje remnants were fully integrated into Tang and Silla.

v.t: Pei Xingjian puts down a Turkic rebellion
v.s: The Eastern Turkic tribes led by Ashide Wenfu and Ashina Fuzhi rose in rebellion, proclaiming Ashina Nishufu as khan, with twenty-four prefectures of Turkic tribes responding. Emperor Gaozong appointed Pei Xingjian as commander-in-chief of the Dingxiang route with 300,000 troops to suppress the rebellion. Pei Xingjian set ambushes around Shuozhou and won a succession of victories, capturing Nishufu and killing Wenfu. This campaign intimidated the remaining Eastern Turkic tribes and temporarily stabilized the northern frontier.

v.t: Wu Zetian's power continues to grow
v.s: Taking advantage of Gaozong's illness and factional strife among ministers, Wu Zetian vigorously promoted her own followers (such as Wu Chengsi and Wu Sansi) while denouncing and exiling opponents (such as Pei Yan and Liu Yizhi). Wu also established the 'North Gate Scholars' (so called because they were summoned in the north gate of the palace) as her brain trust. Court power had completely fallen into the hands of Wu Zetian, and Gaozong was effectively sidelined.

v.t: Wang Bo drowns
v.s: Wang Bo, the leading poet of the 'Four Elites of the Early Tang', drowned in a storm while traveling to Jiaozhi (present-day northern Vietnam) to visit his father. He was only 27 years old. Wang Bo's masterpiece, 'A Tribute to the Tengwang Tower', with its famous line 'Falling glow flies together with the lonely duck; autumn waters share one color with the vast sky', has been celebrated through the ages. His early death was a great loss to Tang poetry.

v.t: Shia Islam formally takes shape
v.s: Husayn ibn Ali, the son of Ali, led a rebellion against the Umayyads and was surrounded by Umayyad forces at Karbala (in present-day Iraq). Husayn and his seventy-two followers were all killed, and Husayn's head was sent to Damascus. The Battle of Karbala caused a permanent split between Shia and Sunni Islam. The Shia venerate Husayn as the third imam and commemorate the event annually on the Day of Ashura. Shia identity was now fixed around the doctrine of the Imamate.

v.t: Establishment of the Second Turkic Khaganate (Göktürk Khaganate)
v.s: A Turkic chieftain named Ilterish Qaghan (also known as Kutluk or Tonyukuk) rose in rebellion in the Mongolian plateau, recovering the Iron (Tiele) tribes previously under Tang control, and re-established the Turkic Khaganate, known as the 'Second Turkic Khaganate' or 'Göktürk Khaganate'. Ilterish established his capital at Ötüken (present-day Hangai Mountains, Mongolia) and frequently raided Tang's northern borders. The Second Turkic Khaganate lasted for over half a century until its destruction by the Uyghurs in 744.

v.t: Establishment of the First Bulgarian Empire
v.s: The Bulgar chieftain Asparukh led his people across the Danube and defeated a Byzantine army, establishing the First Bulgarian Empire, with its capital at Pliska. Byzantine Emperor Constantine IV was forced to sign a treaty recognizing Bulgaria's independence and paying annual tribute. The First Bulgarian Empire became a major power in the Balkans, lasting until 1018.

v.t: Pei Xingjian again defeats the Turks
v.s: The remaining Turkic rebel Ashina Fuzhi proclaimed himself khan and resisted the Tang. Pei Xingjian again led an expedition, using a strategy of sowing discord to split the rebels. Fuzhi was captured and handed over to the Tang. Pei executed Fuzhi, and the Turkic rebellion was completely crushed. Thereafter, the Tang's northern border enjoyed relative peace for about a decade.

v.t: Arabs pursue Arabization policies
v.s: The Umayyad Caliphate enforced the use of Arabic as the official language of administration and religion in conquered territories such as Syria, Iraq, and Egypt, and encouraged Arabs to migrate to new territories and integrate with conquered peoples. Non-Arab Muslims (mawali) were required to form alliances with Arabs but still faced discrimination. These policies accelerated the spread of Islam and the formation of Arab culture, but also led to strong resentment among peoples such as the Persians.

v.t: Ilterish Qaghan of the Second Turkic Khaganate invades Tang
v.s: Ilterish Qaghan led his army to capture Tang's Bingzhou (Taiyuan) and Lanzhou (Lan County), plundering population and livestock. The Tang forces fared poorly, and unrest spread across Hebei. Emperor Gaozong was forced to strengthen border defenses and conscript laborers in Hebei to build fortresses.

v.t: The Maya city-state of Yaxchilan reaches its peak
v.s: The Maya city-state of Yaxchilan (in present-day Chiapas, Mexico) flourished under King Itzamnaaj B'alam II (also known as Shield Jaguar II). He undertook grand construction projects, including many pyramids and stelae, recording his campaigns and rituals. Yaxchilan is renowned for its finely carved lintels and hieroglyphic inscriptions, and was an important political and cultural center during the Maya Classic period.

v.t: Emperor Gaozong dies; Emperor Zhongzong ascends the throne
v.s: Emperor Gaozong of Tang died at the age of 56 in the Zhenguan Palace in Luoyang. His will made his son Li Xian emperor (as Emperor Zhongzong), with the provision that in case of urgent military and state affairs, Empress Dowager Wu Zetian would rule. After Zhongzong's accession, he wanted to appoint his father-in-law Wei Xuanzhen as chancellor, which was opposed by Pei Yan. Angrily, Zhongzong said, 'I could give the empire to Wei Xuanzhen, what would it matter?' Wu Zetian used this as a pretext to depose Zhongzong, reducing him to the title of Prince of Luling. She then installed another son, Li Dan, as emperor (Emperor Ruizong).

v.t: Widespread use of Greek fire in Byzantium
v.s: After proving its worth in naval battles, the Byzantine Empire began to widely equip Greek fire projectors and grenades on its navy and in its fortresses. The formula was a state secret and has never been fully reconstructed. Greek fire played a crucial role in the defense of Constantinople against Arab and later Russian attacks, and was a key factor in the Byzantine Empire's millennial endurance.

v.t: Wu Zetian formally takes the throne as 'Empress Dowager' and holds court
v.s: After Zhongzong was deposed and Ruizong installed, Wu Zetian, as empress dowager, held court and wielded actual power. Emperor Ruizong Li Dan was effectively confined in a side palace and not allowed to participate in government. Wu changed the era name to Wenming and renamed the eastern capital Luoyang as Shendu (Divine Capital). She began comprehensive preparations for declaring herself emperor.

v.t: Xu Jingye rebels against Wu Zetian at Yangzhou
v.s: Xu Jingye (grandson of Li Ji, later renamed Li Jingye), incensed by his demotion, raised an army at Yangzhou with Luo Binwang and Du Qiuren, ostensibly to restore the deposed Prince of Luling Li Xian. Luo Binwang wrote the famous manifesto 'Opposing Wu Zhao's Imperial Usurpation', which was widely circulated for its literary flair. Wu Zetian sent Li Xiaoyi with 300,000 troops to suppress the rebellion. Xu Jingye was defeated and killed. This was the largest opposition to Wu before she declared herself emperor, and after its failure she was free to pursue her usurpation of the Tang throne.

v.t: Wu Zetian deposes Zhongzong, elevates Ruizong, and assumes regency
v.s: After deposing Li Xian, Wu Zetian installed her fourth son Li Dan as Emperor Ruizong, but he was confined and not allowed to attend court. Wu changed the era name to Guangzhai, ordered the removal of the Tang imperial clan from the imperial register, changed the colours of flags and officials' robes, and declared a general amnesty. She also renamed the eastern capital Luoyang as Shendu (Divine Capital), paving the way for her assumption of the throne.

v.t: Umayyads attack Khwarazm
v.s: The Arab governor of Khurasan, Qutayba ibn Muslim, led an expedition against Khwarazm (western Uzbekistan and northern Turkmenistan). Qutayba conquered Khwarazm, forcing its king to submit and pay tribute. Arab power in Central Asia expanded further, threatening the Tang Anxi Protectorate.

v.t: Wu Zetian encourages informants and uses harsh officials
v.s: To eliminate political enemies and potential opponents, Wu Zetian ordered that 'informants, no matter what their status, shall not be interrogated, but shall be provided with post horses and given a grade-five official's daily allowance'. Informants, regardless of status, were granted an audience. Wu heavily relied on harsh officials like Lai Junchen and Zhou Xing, creating many miscarriages of justice and executing hundreds of Tang imperial clan members and ministers. The court was filled with terror, and Wu Zetian thereby solidified her power.

v.t: The Bulgars formally establish a state
v.s: The Bulgars under Asparukh, having defeated the Byzantines, formally established the First Bulgarian Empire. The Byzantine Empire was forced to recognize its independence and pay an annual tribute. The Bulgars gradually became Slavicized, forming the Bulgarian nation.

v.t: Silla sends an envoy to Tang to request books
v.s: King Sinmun of Silla sent a mission to Chang'an to request Buddhist sutras, Confucian classics, and works on astronomy and calendrical science. The Tang emperor (under Wu Zetian's control) granted the request, awarding many books. This promoted cultural development in Silla; the Unified Silla period created a Confucian education system modeled on the Tang.

v.t: Wu Zetian builds the Bright Hall (Mingtang)
v.s: Wu Zetian had the Bright Hall (Mingtang) built in Luoyang as a ritual building for sacrificing to heaven and earth and for promulgating government decrees. The Bright Hall was 294 chi high, with three floors, richly decorated. Wu Zetian held many ceremonies and court meetings there, to proclaim her legitimacy as having received the Mandate of Heaven, preparing public opinion for her assumption of the throne.

v.t: Pepin of Herstal becomes Mayor of the Palace in the Frankish kingdom
v.s: Pepin of Herstal (also known as Pepin II), mayor of the palace of the Merovingian kingdom, defeated the Neustrian nobles and unified the entire Frankish kingdom. Although he remained officially a subject of the king, he effectively controlled the military and political power, establishing the tradition of the hereditary mayoralty in the Pepinid family, laying the foundation for the later Carolingian Empire.

v.t: Lai Junchen compiles the 'Art of Framing'
v.s: The harsh official Lai Junchen, together with his associates, compiled the 'Art of Framing' (also known as the 'Informers' Art'), a manual for fabricating charges, creating miscarriages of justice, and extracting confessions. The book had twelve volumes and was extremely cynical. Lai Junchen used his skills to persecute a large number of innocent officials and imperial relatives, but he was eventually executed by Wu Zetian for his crimes.

v.t: The Li imperial clan rises against Wu Zetian
v.s: Several Tang imperial princes rose in a coordinated rebellion against Wu Zetian. Prince Zhen of Yue (Li Zhen) raised troops in Yuzhou (modern Ruyang, Henan) and Prince Chong of Langya (Li Chong) in Bozhou (modern Liaocheng, Shandong) simultaneously. However, they lacked unified command, and were quickly crushed by Wu's forces. Taking advantage of the situation, Wu Zetian massacred the Li clan; dozens of princes, including the Prince of Han, Prince of Lu, and Prince of Huo, were killed, and the Tang imperial clan was almost annihilated.

v.t: Arab armies advance in Africa
v.s: The Arab governor of North Africa led forces westward, conquering the Berber tribes of present-day Morocco. Arab power reached the Atlantic coast, controlling the entire North African coast from Egypt to the Atlantic. The Berbers gradually converted to Islam but retained their own language and tribal structure.

v.t: Silla further improves its state institutions
v.s: King Sinmun of Silla implemented a 'civil service examination' and 'national university' system, modeled on the Tang, establishing a national university (Gukhak) to teach Confucian classics. At the same time, he completed the central and local administrative systems, creating the 'nine provinces and five minor capitals' system (Gu - Ju, So - Gyeong), strengthening centralization. These measures made Unified Silla more stable; Korean historians mark this as the 'beginning of the medieval period'.

v.t: Wu Zetian reforms the official system to prepare for her accession
v.s: Wu Zetian carried out extensive reforms of the official system, renaming the Chancellery as Phoenix Hall, the Gate Department as Swan Court, and the Secretariat as the Ministry for the Advancement of the State. She also changed the names of ministers and officials. She changed the colors of official robes and reformulated official titles, in every way signaling 'reform and renewal'. At the same time, Wu Zetian began to create 'auspicious omens' (such as the discovery of a chart in the Luo River) and used the Buddhist 'Great Cloud Sutra' to proclaim that she was the reincarnation of Maitreya Buddha, preparing public opinion for her ascension as empress regnant.

d.u: Empress Regnant Wu Zetian (Holy Divine Emperor)

v.t: Wu Zetian proclaims herself emperor, establishing the Zhou dynasty
v.s: Wu Zetian, after a petition of support from over 60,000 officials, imperial clan members, chieftains of the Four Directions, and Buddhist and Daoist clergy, formally ascended the throne as 'Emperor Shengshen' (Holy Divine Emperor), changed the name of the dynasty to Zhou, and made the sacred capital (Shendu, Luoyang) her capital. She became the only orthodox female emperor in Chinese history. She used the name Zhou to claim descent from the Zhou dynasty. After becoming emperor, she continued to promote the imperial examinations, elevate commoner-gentry, attack the Guanlong aristocracy, and promote Buddhism. The Zhou dynasty ruled for a total of fifteen years (690–705).

v.t: Christianity spreads in Nubia
v.s: The three Nubian kingdoms of Nobadia, Makuria, and Alodia (in present-day Sudan) fully converted to Christian Monophysitism under the influence of Byzantine missionaries. Churches and monasteries were built, and Greek and Old Nubian were used for writing. Christianity endured in Nubia for nearly a thousand years, until the Islamic conquest of the 14th century.

d.u: Empress Regnant Wu Zetian (Holy Divine Emperor)

v.t: Wu Zetian moves the capital to Luoyang
v.s: Wu Zetian formally moved the political center from Chang'an to Luoyang, renaming it the Divine Capital (Shendu). She made Luoyang the capital of the Zhou dynasty, with Chang'an as a secondary capital. Luoyang, located in the Central Plains, was convenient for transportation and far from the old Guanlong aristocracy. Wu Zetian undertook large-scale construction in Luoyang, building the Heavenly Hall, the Bright Hall, the Tianjin Bridge, and other magnificent structures, making Luoyang one of the most prosperous cities in the world.

v.t: Arab Empire builds the Dome of the Rock
v.s: The Umayyad caliph Abd al-Malik completed the Dome of the Rock on the Temple Mount in Jerusalem. This building used copious gold and Byzantine-style mosaics, and is considered a masterpiece of Islamic architecture. The construction was partly intended to rival the religious centrality of the Kaaba in Mecca (since Abd al-Malik was in conflict with anti-Umayyad forces in Mecca), but it remains a holy site.

d.u: Empress Regnant Wu Zetian (Holy Divine Emperor)

v.t: Wang Xiaojie recaptures the Anxi Four Garrisons for Wu Zhou
v.s: Wu Zetian appointed Wang Xiaojie as commander-in-chief of the Wuwei route, leading an expedition against Tibet. Wang defeated the Tibetans at Qinghai and in the Western Regions, recapturing the four garrisons of Kucha, Kashgar, Khotan, and Karashahr. Wu Zetian re-established the Anxi Protectorate at Kucha, restoring Tang control over the Western Regions. Wang Xiaojie was promoted to General of the Left Guard for his success.

v.t: Justinian II is restored to the Byzantine throne
v.s: After being deposed and exiled, Byzantine Emperor Justinian II, with the help of the Bulgars and Slavs, led an army into Constantinople and overthrew the usurper Leontios, regaining the throne. His restoration began with a bloody purge, leading to a reign of terror that ended with his second overthrow in 695.

d.u: Empress Regnant Wu Zetian (Holy Divine Emperor)

v.t: Wu Zetian bans slaughter and promotes Buddhism
v.s: Wu Zetian, a devout Buddhist, ordered a prohibition on the slaughter of animals and the catching of fish to demonstrate compassion. This affected folk dietary and sacrificial customs. At the same time, she supported Buddhist scripture translation and temple construction, so that Buddhism reached its peak during the Wu Zhou period. The Northern Chan master Shenxiu was welcomed into Chang'an and honored as 'National Preceptor'.

d.u: Empress Regnant Wu Zetian (Holy Divine Emperor)

v.t: Manichaeism enters China
v.s: A Persian Manichaean missionary named Fuduo (or Fotudeng) arrived in Chang'an with Manichaean scriptures and was received by Wu Zetian. Manichaeism, which teaches the struggle between Light and Darkness (two principles and three ages), was allowed to spread because Wu Zetian saw its usefulness for legitimizing her rule. Manichaeism was later banned during the Tang Wuzong's persecution of Buddhism, but it continued among the Uyghurs.

v.t: Arab forces capture Carthage
v.s: The Arab general Hassan ibn al-Nu'man led 40,000 troops to besiege Carthage again. The Byzantine defenders and Berber allies resisted, but the city eventually fell. Carthage was completely destroyed to prevent the Byzantines from reusing it. The Arabs then established Tunis as the new capital of North Africa. The ancient city of Carthage disappeared from history.

d.u: Empress Regnant Wu Zetian (Holy Divine Emperor)

v.t: Wu Zetian adds the title 'Tiance Golden Wheel Holy Divine Emperor'
v.s: To enhance her authority, Wu Zetian accepted from her ministers the additional honorific title 'Tiance Golden Wheel Holy Divine Emperor'. She changed her era name several times (Zhengsheng, Tiance Wansui, Wansui Dengfeng, etc.), and held grand ceremonies such as the Fengshan at Mount Song and sacrificing to the Luo River. She also added the Buddhist emblem of the 'Golden Wheel' to her title, emphasizing that she was a cakravartin (wheel-turning king), strengthening the notion that her rule was divinely ordained.

v.t: Umayyads establish the governorate of Ifriqiya
v.s: The Arab Empire formally established the governorate of Ifriqiya (northwest Africa from Egypt to Morocco) with its capital at Kairouan. Hassan ibn al-Nu'man was appointed the first governor. This administrative division strengthened Arab rule over North Africa and paved the way for the crossing of the Strait of Gibraltar and the conquest of Spain.

d.u: Empress Regnant Wu Zetian (Holy Divine Emperor)
d.e: 1st year of the Wansui Tongtian era

v.t: Khitan chief Li Jinzhong rebels against Wu Zhou
v.s: The Khitan tribal chief Li Jinzhong (the Li surname was a Tang honorific), angered by mistreatment by the Wu Zhou official Zhao Wenju, joined with Sun Wanrong to rise in rebellion, capturing Yingzhou (present-day Chaoyang, Liaoning). Li Jinzhong declared himself 'Wushang Khan'. Wu Zetian sent generals Cao Renshi and Zhang Xuanyu to suppress the rebellion, but they were annihilated at the Huangzhong Valley. Later, the Turks raided the Khitan rear, Li Jinzhong was killed, and Sun Wanrong fled to the Turks and was killed. This rebellion severely damaged Wu Zhou's control over the northeast.

v.t: Rise of Qapaghan Qaghan of the Second Turkic Khaganate
v.s: After the death of Ilterish Qaghan, his brother Qapaghan Qaghan ascended the throne, taking the title 'Qian Shan Qaghan' (or 'Kül Qaghan'). Qapaghan, a talented leader, quickly united the Turkic tribes, conquering the Tiele, Khitans, and other tribes. He raided the Tang repeatedly, forcing Wu Zetian to cede land and give gifts. Under Qapaghan, the Second Turkic Khaganate reached its peak, becoming the greatest northern threat to the Tang.

v.t: The Frankish kingdom allies with the Pope
v.s: The Frankish mayor of the palace, Pepin of Herstal, established an alliance with Pope Sergius I. The pope sought Frankish support due to persecution by the Byzantine emperor over iconoclasm. This alliance foreshadowed the later Donation of Pepin and the coronation of Charlemagne, marking the start of a long alliance between the papacy and the Frankish monarchy.

d.u: Empress Regnant Wu Zetian (Holy Divine Emperor)

v.t: Wu Zetian executes Lai Junchen
v.s: The harsh official Lai Junchen planned to accuse the Wu princes and Princess Taiping of rebellion, but they united to expose his crimes. Wu Zetian ordered Lai Junchen executed. The death of Lai Junchen marked the end of the stage of 'cruel official rule' under Wu Zetian, bringing some relief to the court.

v.t: Japan begins compiling the 'Taihō Code'
v.s: Emperor Monmu of Japan, under the leadership of Chancellor Fujiwara no Fuhito, promulgated the 'Taihō Code' (the era name was Taihō). This code included criminal law (ritsu) and administrative law (ryō), modeled on the Tang 'Yonghui Code' but adapted to Japanese conditions. The Taihō Code perfected the 'Two Departments and Eight Ministries' central bureaucracy, divided the country into 66 provinces, and implemented the Handen Shūju (rice field allotment) and household registration systems. It marked the formal establishment of Japan as a law-based state, with influence lasting for centuries.

d.u: Empress Regnant Wu Zetian (Holy Divine Emperor)

v.t: Wu Zhou appoints Li Xian as crown prince
v.s: Heeding the advice of Di Renjie and other ministers, Wu Zetian abandoned the idea of making her nephew Wu Sansi crown prince, and secretly brought the deposed Li Xian (Prince of Luling) back to Luoyang, making him crown prince. This marked Wu Zetian's final decision to return the throne to the Li imperial family, setting the stage for the Shenlong Coup and the restoration of the Tang.

v.t: Umayyad campaign against Byzantium
v.s: Umayyad caliph Abd al-Malik sent forces to attack Byzantine Asia Minor, seizing parts of Isauria and Cappadocia. Emperor Tiberius III reached a peace agreement with the Arabs, paying an annual tribute. The Byzantine Empire's eastern defenses continued to contract.

d.u: Empress Regnant Wu Zetian (Holy Divine Emperor)

v.t: Qapaghan Qaghan invades Hebei
v.s: Qapaghan Qaghan led 100,000 cavalry south, capturing Dingzhou and Zhaozhou, killing and plundering. Wu Zetian sent Di Renjie, Li Xian, and others to lead resistance. Di Renjie used a scorched-earth strategy, and the Turks withdrew when their supplies ran out. Thereafter, the Tang shifted its defense focus to Hebei, constructing fortifications to guard against the Turks.

v.t: Tang adjusts its frontier defenses
v.s: In response to threats from the Turks, Tibet, and the Khitans, Wu Zetian reorganized the northern defense system. She set up major garrisons such as the Tianbing Army and the Dawu Army, strengthening the defenses of Taiyuan and Youzhou. At the same time, she used marriage alliances, giving a Tang princess in marriage to Qapaghan Qaghan, temporarily easing the situation.

d.u: Empress Regnant Wu Zetian (Holy Divine Emperor)

v.t: Di Renjie dies
v.s: The renowned chancellor Di Renjie of the Wu Zhou died of illness at the age of 71. Wu Zetian said mournfully, 'The court is empty now.' She posthumously honored him as Minister of Education (Wenchang Youxiang) with the posthumous name Wenhui. Di Renjie was known for his uprightness and forthright remonstrations, often dissuading Wu Zetian from excessive killing and military adventures, and protecting the Li imperial clan. He secretly recommended officials such as Zhang Jianzhi to Wu, which paved the way for the Shenlong Coup that restored the Tang dynasty.

v.t: Christianity spreads among the Germanic peoples
v.s: The Anglo-Saxon missionary Willibrord, with the support of the Frankish mayor of the palace Pepin of Herstal, went to Frisia (modern Netherlands) and the German lands. He established the bishopric of Utrecht, leading many Germanic tribes to convert to Christianity. This was an important step in the spread of Christianity to northern and eastern Europe.

v.t: Ancestral Puebloans build cliff dwellings in North America
v.s: Around 700–1300 AD, the Ancestral Puebloans (also known as Anasazi) in the southwestern United States built multi-story stone villages in cliffs, such as at Mesa Verde and Chaco Canyon. At Chaco Canyon, they built 12 large 'great houses', the largest, Pueblo Bonito, having over 600 rooms. These buildings were precisely aligned to the sun and moon. Around 1300, due to severe drought, the Puebloans migrated south to the Rio Grande valley.

v.t: First Polynesian settlers arrive on Easter Island
v.s: Around 700–900 AD, Polynesian navigators arrived on Easter Island (Rapa Nui) in double-hulled canoes, one of the most remote inhabited islands on Earth. Over several centuries, the islanders built about 900 moai statues, the tallest 10 meters high and weighing 82 tons. The moai represented ancestral spirits and were placed on stone platforms called ahu. Deforestation and resource overuse led to social collapse and civil war; by the time Europeans arrived, the population had greatly declined.
